coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots/hydrogel nanocomposite with stable Fe3+ sensing capability
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Chaoge
Xing, Malcolm
Wu, Qilin
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel fluorescent smart hydrogel based on carbon dots (CDs)/hydrogel nanocomposite material for stable Fe3+ ion detection was prepared. The CDs were firstly modified by polyethyleneimine (PEI) and then immersed into microcrystalline cellulose (MCC) hydrogel. The obtained PEI-CDs were well characterized for their morphological features, chemical composition and optical properties by transmission electron microscopy and different spectroscopic analysis. The smart hydrogel nanocomposite was characterized by scanning electron microscopy, fluorescence spectrophotometer, infrared spectroscopy as well as thermogravimetric analysis. The results revealed that the prepared PEI-CDs were spherical in shape and monodispersed in water with a quantum yield of 28%. The smart PEI-CDs/MCC hydrogel exhibited a better photo-stability than single PEI-CDs by avoiding their self-quenching. Moreover, there existed interactions between PEI-CDs and MCC hydrogel, which could enhance the cross-linking degree of MCC hydrogel, resulting in improved mechanical and thermal properties of nanocomposite. This easy prepared fluorescent smart hydrogel is an attractive material for stable Fe3+ ion sensing application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanostructured materials
Optical materials
Sol-gel processes
Luminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of MCC hydrogel and fluorescent smart hydrogel
Fluorescence sensing of Fe3+ ion with smart hydrogel

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic procedure of (a) CDs, PEI-CDs and (b) fluorescent smart hydrogel.

(a) The TEM image and size distribution (inset) of PEI-CDs. (b) FT-IR spectra of CDs and PEI-CDs. (c) UV–Vis absorption and PL emission spectra of CDs and PEI-CDs (The PL emission spectra were normalized by intensity; Inset: photograph of the aqueous solution of CDs and PEI-CDs illuminated by a UV lamp with 360 nm light). (d) PL emission spectra of PEI-CDs at different pH values.

(a) The gelation process of MCC solution. (b) Photograph and FE-SEM image of MCC hydrogel. (c) Photograph and FE-SEM image of smart hydrogel. (d) PL emission spectra of MCC hydrogel and smart hydrogel (insets are the photographs of MCC hydrogel and smart hydrogel under daylight and UV light). (e) The homogeneous characterization of PEI-CDs in smart hydrogel. (f) Effect of UV irradiation time on PL intensity of PEI-CDs and fluorescent smart hydrogel.
(a) The FT-IR spectra of PEI-CDs, MCC hydrogel and fluorescent smart hydrogel. (b) TGA analysis of MCC hydrogel and fluorescent smart hydrogel.

(a) PL spectra and (b) PL relative intensity of the fluorescent smart hydrogel in the presence of different concentration of Fe3+ ion (inset shows the PL quenching photographs of smart hydrogel with increasing Fe3+ ion concentrations). (c) The linear relationship of F0/F versus the concentration of Fe3+ ion over the range of 10–100 μM. The determination of Fe3+ ion using the smart hydrogel for (d) tap water, (e) lake water and (f) sewage.
Comparison of the fluorescent probe based on CDs for the detection of Fe3+ ions.
Determination of Fe3+ ion using the smart hydrogel for various water samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The Fe3+ amounts in the tap water, lake water, and sewage are measured by AAS (atomic adsorption spectroscopy).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Measured amounts determined by the smart hydrogel as fluorescence probe; it is an average value of the response of five measurements for each sample.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots/hydrogel nanocomposite with stable Fe3+ sensing capability

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for Modification of Chemical Fibers and Polymer Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Donghua University, Shanghai 201620, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Modification of Chemical Fibers and Polymer Materials
College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemistry and Medical Genetics, University of Manitoba, Winnipeg, Manitoba R3T 5V6, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biochemistry and Medical Genetics

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Materials Science and Engineering, Donghua University, 2999 North Renmin Road, Songjiang District, Shanghai, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Biochemistry and Medical Genetics, University of Manitoba, Winnipeg, Manitoba R3T 5V6, Canada.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biochemistry and Medical Genetics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a novel fluorescent smart hydrogel based on carbon dots (CDs)/hydrogel nanocomposite material for stable Fe3+ ion detection was prepared. The CDs were firstly modified by polyethyleneimine (PEI) and then immersed into microcrystalline cellulose (MCC) hydrogel. The obtained PEI-CDs were well characterized for their morphological features, chemical composition and optical properties by transmission electron microscopy and different spectroscopic analysis. The smart hydrogel nanocomposite was characterized by scanning electron microscopy, fluorescence spectrophotometer, infrared spectroscopy as well as thermogravimetric analysis. The results revealed that the prepared PEI-CDs were spherical in shape and monodispersed in water with a quantum yield of 28%. The smart PEI-CDs/MCC hydrogel exhibited a better photo-stability than single PEI-CDs by avoiding their self-quenching. Moreover, there existed interactions between PEI-CDs and MCC hydrogel, which could enhance the cross-linking degree of MCC hydrogel, resulting in improved mechanical and thermal properties of nanocomposite. This easy prepared fluorescent smart hydrogel is an attractive material for stable Fe3+ ion sensing application.

ce:section.ce:para: Development of effective fluorescent sensors for Fe3+ ion detection is an attractive long-term analytical topic [1–3]. Up to now, a variety of instrumental techniques for the detection of Fe3+ ion have been established [4–6]. These instrumental techniques exhibited high accuracy and wide detection range for Fe3+ ion detection. However, they usually involve sophisticated instrumentation and expensive facilities. Recently, more and more researches are focus on easier and cheaper fluorescent liquid nanotechnological platforms [2,7,8], which also encounter some problems. For example, most existing carbon dots (CDs) based fluorometric methods for Fe3+ ion detection use a single CDs aqueous solution as the standard indicator. But these CDs trend to agglomerate as the testing time rises, which will cause erroneous measurements [9].

One possible solution to overcome this problem is to develop self-assembly fluorescent sensors in other media, for example, hydrogels. The suitable hydrogels can hold the fluorescent sensors and avoid sensors aggregation [10,11]. Recently, some papers have been published to prepare new functional sensor probe by combining hydrogels and fluorescent Nano sensors [12–15]. For example, Celia [12] et al. constructed a fluorescent nanocellulosic hydrogel by combining nanocellulose and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for laccase monitoring. The hydrogel matrix not only increased and stabilized the fluorescence signal of GQDs but also improved the sensitivity of GQDs towards laccase. Microcrystalline cellulose (MCC), as a natural gelation precursor, is a promising matrix to immobilize the fluorescence sensors for its fascinating biodegradable character and abundant surface hydroxyl groups [16]. We believe that MCC can be a good fluorescence sensor matrix for Fe3+ ion detection.

By virtue of the high biocompatibility, easy modification and low fabrication cost, CDs, especially the biowaste based CDs, recently seem to be an eligible candidate for fluorescence-based sensing of heavy metal ions, such as Fe3+ [1,17], Pb2+ [18], Hg2+ [19–21], Cu2+ [22,23], etc. The prominent features of the CDs are mainly determined by the carbon source [24,25]. The biowaste based CDs always involves less functionality and low quantum yield due to their poor structural regularity and uniformity as carbonaceous source. These defects limit the use of CDs in sensor probe applications. Therefore, the surface of CDs is often modified by using some organic molecules or polymers to improve the photoluminescent (PL) performance, such as polyethylene glycol [26], poly(N-vinylcaprolactam) [27] and polyethyleneimine (PEI) [28], etc. Among them, PEI is a hydrophilic polymer with numerous active groups on its molecular structure, which can generate strong hydrogen bonding with organic compounds containing oxygen- or nitrogen-functional groups [29]. When immobilized in a hydrogel matrix, it is expected that CDs modified by PEI can enhance PL performance and increase the interaction between the CDs and the hydrogel matrix.

Herein, we are interested in producing smart hydrogel containing two types of biocompatible materials for Fe3+ ion detection, one acting as the fluorescent sensor, and the other as the gel matrix to eliminate self-quenching of the fluorescent sensor. We firstly prepared fluorescent sensor PEI-CDs by using willow catkin and PEI as carbon source and surface passivation reagent. Then, we have developed a simple method to prepare a fluorescent smart hydrogel composed of PEI-CDs embedded in the MCC matrix. The morphology, structure and PL features of PEI-CDs and smart hydrogel nanocomposite were investigated. In addition, the interaction between PEI-CDs and MCC hydrogel was confirmed. The Fe3+ sensing capability of synthesized smart hydrogel has been explored in real water samples as well.

The pristine CDs were synthesized by the burning treatment of catkins. Specifically, willow catkin (5 g) was firstly soaked in 1000 mL of N/S aqueous solution containing 12 g of urea and 35 mL of sulfuric acid (98%) for 12 h. After that, the soaked willow catkin was dried at 60 °C for another 4 h and then completely burned to ashes in the air. The ashes were dispersed in 1 L ultrapure water and then centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant liquid containing CDs was collected and preserved.

In order to improve the quantum yield and functionality of CDs, the PEI-CDs were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal treatment of PEI and CDs aqueous solution (Fig. 1 a). The effects of reaction conditions during the surface passivation of CDs, such as PEI concentrations, hydrothermal temperature and reaction time on the PL properties of PEI-CDs were investigated and an optimized reaction condition was identified (Fig. S1). Typically, a certain amount (5 mg) of PEI was dissolved in 30 mL of CDs aqueous solution. Then, the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel vessel for hydrothermal treatment (200 °C for 6 h). Afterward, the products were centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min to remove large particles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of MCC hydrogel and fluorescent smart hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: A facile approach to prepare MCC hydrogel was developed by using ethanol as cross-linker (Fig. 1b). Briefly, 3 g of MCC powder was gently added to a mixed solution containing ultrapure water (50 mL), sodium hydroxide (3 g) and urea (6 g), which was pre-cooled for 1 h at −18 °C. Then, the above solution was mechanically stirred at room temperature for at least 1 h to allow complete dissolution of MCC. Then, ethanol serving as a cross-linker was added drop-wise to the above system and kept for 10 min to perform the cross-linking reaction. The resulting hydrogel was then rinsed repeatedly with Millipore water to remove excess reactants until a neutral pH was reached. Finally, the hydrogel was stored in water before use.

Similarly, for synthesis of fluorescent smart hydrogel, a certain number of PEI-CDs were added to MCC mixture and well mixed before gelation. The final concentration of PEI-CDs in MCC mixture is 0.5 mg/mL.

For fluorescent sensing of Fe3+ ion, the smart hydrogel was immersed in target aqueous samples containing desired concentration (0–140 μM) of Fe3+ ion. Afterward, the PL spectra of hydrogel were obtained after shaking for thirty seconds and recorded using an FP-6600 spectrophotometer at 360 nm excitation. The selectivity of smart hydrogel toward Fe3+ ion was evaluated by adding other common metal ions (including Co2+, Al3+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Pb2+, and Ni2+, 100 μM) instead of Fe3+ ion (100 μM).

In order to evaluate the practicality of fluorescent smart hydrogel, the smart hydrogel was applied for assaying Fe3+ ion in three different real samples (tap water, lake water and sewage) using standard addition method. Lake water was obtained from Jingyue Lake and sewage was collected from a small sewer near the garbage collection station (Fig. S2). All the above water samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and spiked with different quantity of Fe3+ ion before analysis. Then, the fluorescent smart hydrogel was added to the above water samples. Afterward, the PL spectra of hydrogel were obtained after shaking for thirty seconds and recorded using an FP-6600 spectrophotometer at 360 nm excitation.

The obtained CDs (Fig. S3a) and PEI-CDs (Fig. 3a) were found to be nearly spherical in morphology, and the average diameters of CDs and PEI-CDs were around 7.3 nm and 7.8 nm, respectively. Unlike CDs, the PEI-CDs presented a slight agglomeration because the abundant hydrophilic groups on PEI could cause complex interactions among PEI-CDs [28]. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern in Fig. S4 indicated the poor crystalline nature of the PEI-CDs [30]. Moreover, the XRD pattern of PEI-CDs as shown in Fig. S5 exhibited a broad amorphous peak centered at 2θ ∼24°, and additional weak peak at 2θ ∼40° [31]. The obtained XRD pattern implied the amorphous feature of PEI-CDs, and indicated that a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups are present on PEI-CDs [30]. The chemical components of both CDs and PEI-CDs were investigated by FT-IR (Fig. 2 b) and XPS (Fig. S3b). The two samples both displayed the characteristic bands of COH, CH/CN and CC at 1098 cm−1, 1400 cm−1 and 1637 cm−1, respectively [13,32,33]. Compared with pristine CDs, the PEI-CDs showed an increase in the relative element contents of carbon and nitrogen (Fig. S3b). In addition, it should be noted that, after modified by PEI, the CDs showed increased NH band and primary amines (−NH2) band at 3116 cm−1 and 1463 cm−1, respectively [34]. These results combined with XPS analysis (Fig. S3b and Fig. S6) indicated that the PEI-CDs were successfully prepared by hydrothermal method.

The PL features of both CDs and PEI-CDs were investigated by fluorescence and ultraviolet spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2c, under excitation at 360 nm, the aqueous solutions of CDs and PEI-CDs exhibited strong blue emission at 417 nm and 445 nm, respectively. Unlike the absorption peak of CDs at 280 nm (attributed to π–π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains) [20], the PEI-CDs had a absorption peak at 325 nm (attributed to n–π* transition). After PEI modification, the CDs’ aqueous solution color changed from light to deep yellow (Fig. S7), which may due to the changes in the surface properties and electronic transitions of the PEI-CDs. Additionally, it was also found that the prepared PEI-CDs exhibited a high quantum yield of 28%, which was more than doubled compared to original CDs (Fig. S8). Most importantly, as shown in Fig. 2d, PEI-CD was found to have excellent photo-stability over a wide pH range (3–8), which would facilitate their use in sensing and bioimaging applications [24].

PEI molecules can play two key roles in the smart hydrogel system, on one hand, as a nitrogen-rich surface passivator to enhance the PL properties of CDs, on the other hand, as an amino-rich linker to enhance the cross-linking degree of smart hydrogel. The gelation process of MCC after introduction of PEI-CDs was studied and evaluated (Fig. 3a). The result showed that the smart hydrogel exhibited much higher entanglement network density than pure MCC hydrogel. In addition, the FE-SEM images of these two different types of hydrogel (Fig. 3b–c) proved that PEI-CDs helped to increase the cross-linking degree of MCC hydrogel, which may further support the gel structure and immobilize the fluorescence sensors.

More interestingly, unlike the MCC hydrogel, which has no obvious fluorescent emission under 360 nm excitation, the smart hydrogel displayed a strong blue emission band centered at 445 nm (Fig. 3d). As shown in Fig. 3e, there was no obvious variation in PL intensity among different areas (areas number 1–8) of the smart hydrogel, indicating that the PEI-CDs can be well dispersed in the smart hydrogel. Simultaneously, the photo-stability of PEI-CDs and fluorescent smart hydrogel were measured at 360 nm excitation (150 W UV-lamp) for different time periods and the results were presented in Fig. 3f. It was demonstrated that the PEI-CDs conjugated MCC hydrogel was more stable after long term excitation. The above phenomenon may be due to the highly 3D porous structure of smart hydrogel (Fig. 3c), which avoids the PEI-CDs aggregation and enhance their photo-stability to some extent [13].

The FT-IR spectra of PEI-CDs, MCC hydrogel, and fluorescent smart hydrogel were recorded in Fig. 4 a and used to investigate the interaction between PEI-CDs and MCC hydrogel matrix. These three cellulose-derived samples all shared similar absorption peaks, indicating that the fluorescent smart hydrogel retained the main functional groups of MCC after incorporation of PEI-CDs. However, compared to pure PEI-CDs and MCC hydrogel, the broad OH peak region near to 3,400 cm−1 and COH peak region around 1,068 cm−1 of the smart hydrogel were shifted to lower wavenumbers. Moreover, these two bands were broadened after PEI-CDs was added into MCC hydrogel. These results demonstrated that there existed strong hydrogen bonding interactions between hydrophilic hydroxide radicals on the surface of the MCC and the OH groups of the PEI-CDs [35]. As depicted in Fig. 4b, the thermal stability of MCC Hydrogel and fluorescent smart hydrogel were analyzed by TGA. Both of two samples exhibited a two-stage thermal events; The first event (around 40–180 °C) was attributed to the loss of adsorbed water molecules, whilst the thermal decomposition of MCC and PEI- CDs were regarded as the second event (around 200–350 °C) [16]. After PEI-CDs incorporation, the fluorescent MCC hydrogel displayed higher weight-loss (9.2%) in the first event than pure MCC hydrogel (6.9%) and a slight enhancement of thermal stability, which may ascribed to high hydrophilic group loading and increased interfacial interactions between PEI- CDs and MCC hydrogel [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of Fe3+ ion with smart hydrogel

ce:section.ce:para: To explore the practical application of fluorescent smart hydrogel for the detection of Fe3+ ion in real water samples, we applied them to different three water samples including tap water, lake water and sewage. Since the real water samples might not contain Fe3+ ion, they were spiked with 20, 40, or 60 μM Fe3+ ion in advance for subsequent detection. As shown in Fig. 5d–f and Table 2 , the measured amounts of Fe3+ ion in tap water and lake water were relatively close to that of the spiked amounts, and the recoveries was high (from 97.3% to 101.2%). The measured amounts of tap water and lake water showed low RSD% of 1.64% and 1.92%, respectively. The above results showed that the as-synthesized fluorescent smart hydrogel has higher accuracy and stability than other works in detecting Fe3+ ion in environmental samples [42,43]. More importantly, the measured amounts of Fe3+ ion in sewage were 4.26 μM, which is relatively close to the Fe3+ amounts tested by AAS (3.73 μM). When the sewage is further contaminated by different quantity of Fe3+ ion, good recoveries ranging from 101.5% to 104.8% can also be obtained, suggesting the applicability of the proposed method to real samples. The good performance of the prepared fluorescent smart hydrogel may be due to the presence of MCC hydrogel, which may avoid the aggregation of PEI-CDs during the detection process.

In this study, we fabricated a fluorescent smart PEI-CDs/hydrogel nanocomposite with Fe3+ sensing capability based on PEI-CDs as the luminophore towards Fe3+ ion. Compared with a single PEI-CDs for Fe3+ ion detection, the easily-prepared smart hydrogel nanocomposite improved the photo-stability of PEI-CDs by avoiding their self-quenching. The obtained smart hydrogel showed an intense blue emission at 445 nm with high stability. Importantly, the intensity of emission signal displayed a good linear relationship with Fe3+ ion concentration in the range of 10–100 μM. The correlation coefficient was 0.9965 and the limit of detection was 65 nM. In addition, the smart hydrogel can be used as fluorescent probe with good accuracy and stability for Fe3+ ion detection in real water samples, which may open a new direction for Fe3+ ion detection application.
The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support by National Key R&D Program of China [grant numbers 2016YFB0303201]; the Research and Innovation Project of Shanghai Municipal Education Commission [grant numbers 14zz069]; M.X. would like to thank the Discovery Grant of National Science and Engineering Research Council of Canada (NSERC) and NSERC Discovery Accelerator Supplement Award.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2019.03.053.
